text: You remember in the coffee shop simulation where you had to prioritise Friends, Shoppers or Workers? That was a taste of some of the most critical decisions any business owner will ever face: who am I selling to, and where am I going to set up shop?
Let’s look at how you prioritised your target segments. Take Segment A: Friends and Co-Students. The data showed that a massive 89 percent of them wanted to sit in, chat, and access the internet.
If you prioritised friends, did you take these specific habits into account when ranking them? If they were your number one priority, did you plan for a comfortable seating area and free Wi-Fi?
In real-world business, if you do not give this segment a place to hang out, they will walk right past your door.
The simulation gave research from a marketing company. It may be that you have to do your own research, but where your customers go to buy your product is critical.

What about Segment B: Shoppers? They wanted a trusted brand, quality coffee, and comfortable rest stops after carrying heavy bags. Because they represent major mall foot traffic, the best strategic location for them was the South Wing because it had prime retail.

It catches the daily sun and sits right next to three large retail outlets. If you targeted shoppers but placed your shop far away from the retail hub, your business would have struggled to get noticed by your targeted segment.
Then we had Segment C: Office Workers. These customers are completely different. Only 19 percent want to sit in. Instead, 89 percent want a quick buy-and-go takeaway coffee.
Their absolute highest priority is speedy and efficient service at 82 percent.
To capture this market, the ultimate spot was the West Wing, right next to the office complex for morning coffee runs and quick lunches. If you chose this wing, you paid a premium for the location.
But did you budget for high-speed equipment to keep up with their demand for quick service?
In the simulation, a wrong choice just means restarting the game. But in real life, real life does not have a restart button.
You have to listen closely to what your local customers are telling you before you spend your hard-earned capital on rent and stock.
Statistics show over 70 percent of first-time startups fail within one to three years. The simulation has no real-life consequences, so you are just learning.
But in real life you must listen to your customers and deliver what they want to buy, and where they want to buy it.
Look around your local neighbourhood. Where is your West Wing corporate hub? Where is your South Wing retail traffic? And most importantly, who is your target customer?
It may be that your customers are in different neighbourhoods.
